desc: El Nido is the headline act of northern Palawan: a compact, busy beach town wrapped in dark limestone cliffs and used mainly as the launchpad for Bacuit Bay's lagoons, coves and island-hopping routes. It is spectacular when weather and boats align, but it is not a remote-empty paradise in the literal sense; the town itself is dense, transfer-heavy and strongly shaped by tour schedules, small-airport capacity and sea conditions.

todo.name: Bacuit Bay island hopping
todo.desc: Choose a licensed boat tour for lagoons, snorkel stops and limestone scenery, checking conditions on the day.

todo.name: Nacpan Beach
todo.desc: Spend a slower day on the long sand north of town when road conditions are good.

todo.name: Kayaking lagoons
todo.desc: Use kayaks for quieter corners of the lagoons when tour routing allows.

todo.name: Taraw viewpoint area
todo.desc: Pick a safe guided viewpoint or canopy walk rather than attempting risky informal climbs.

todo.name: Seven Commandos or Maremegmeg sunset
todo.desc: Use an easy late-day beach stop for a lower-effort break from boat schedules and tricycle traffic.

todo.name: Lio beach and cafe stop
todo.desc: Spend a slower half-day near the airport side if you want a tidier, less cramped alternative to the main town strip.

wifi.note: Usable in better hotels, cafes and some co-working-oriented stays, but power cuts, weather, island-hopping days and smaller guesthouses make it too patchy to treat as frictionless remote-work infrastructure.

prac.fltNote: El Nido's own Lio airport keeps the final transfer easy but seat supply is limited and usually relies on domestic links via Manila or other Philippine hubs. Puerto Princesa is the usual fallback, but that swaps flight availability for a long van transfer north, so weather, arrival time and tour-day sequencing matter more here than in a standard fly-and-flop beach destination.
prac.lang: Filipino and English; regional languages vary
prac.visa: British citizens can visit the Philippines visa-free for up to 30 days for tourism, but GOV.UK says you need a passport valid for at least 6 months, proof of onward travel, and should complete the Philippines eTravel registration before departure.
alerts: Boat tours are weather and sea-state dependent.
Small-airport seats sell out early in high season.
Town streets are tighter, busier and noisier than many first-time visitors expect from the brochure images.
Cash and backup connectivity help outside higher-end resorts.

sweet: January to March is usually the strongest window for calmer boat conditions, brighter lagoon days and fewer transfer headaches than the wetter middle of the year.
